title: Vector
description: An ordered list of numbers that represents a point or direction in continuous space—embeddings and activations are vectors at different stages of the model.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: A vector is a fixed-length sequence of real numbers. In language models, token embeddings are vectors of model width, and attention layers read and write vectors at every position along the sequence.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: Papers and code often say "vector" when they mean a 1-D tensor: one row from an embedding table, a query vector, or a hidden state. Knowing that vectors are the building blocks inside tensors helps you follow shape notation from embeddings through attention. For shape conventions, broadcasting, and framework dtypes, see the tensor and embedding glossary entries.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: A 768-dimensional embedding for one token is a vector of length 768. Stacking many positions produces a matrix or higher-rank tensor, but each row is still a vector in hidden space.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: A vector is not the same as a sparse one-hot encoding: learned embeddings are dense vectors with values in every dimension. "Dense vector" usually contrasts with sparse bag-of-words features, not with tensors in general.

openingSummary: A vector is an ordered list of numbers; token embeddings and per-position hidden states are vectors that later stack into tensors as they move through the model.
